title: Hypothyroidism
oneLiner: Common endocrine deficiency of thyroid hormones; often insidious, but can cause myxoedema coma – a rare but life-threatening emergency.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Myxoedema coma** – hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mental status, hypoventilation; often triggered by infection, trauma, or cold. **Key finding:** core temp <35°C + depressed GCS + bradycardia (not just "tired patient").
- **Adrenal crisis** in undiagnosed secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary failure) – give thyroid hormone without steroids → acute hypotension/collapse. **Key finding:** hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypotension (rare in primary hypothyroidism; think pituitary).
- **Pericardial effusion/tamponade** – rare but possible in severe chronic hypothyroidism. **Key finding:** low-voltage ECG + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds + reduced pulse pressure.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain (despite poor appetite), constipation, dry skin, hair loss (especially outer third eyebrows), hoarse voice, cognitive slowing ("brain fog"), menorrhagia (premenopausal).
- **Key exam findings:** bradycardia, delayed relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes (especially ankle jerk), dry coarse skin, periorbital oedema, goitre (if Hashimoto’s) or absent thyroid (surgical/radioiodine), non-pitting oedema (myxoedema), slow speech, low body temperature.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Subclinical hypothyroidism:** isolated raised TSH, normal free T4 – asymptomatic or mild symptoms. *Differentiation:* labs, no overt signs.
- **Secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary/hypothalamic):** low TSH, low free T4 – may have other pituitary hormone deficiencies (low cortisol, low LH/FSH). *Differentiation:* check TSH – if it is *not* raised, suspect central cause.
- **Euthyroid sick syndrome (non-thyroidal illness):** low T3/T4 but TSH normal or low – occurs in critical illness (sepsis, MI, starvation). *Differentiation:* clinical context – patient is acutely unwell with other organ failure, resolves with treating underlying illness.
- **Depression / chronic fatigue syndrome:** fatigue, weight change, cognitive slowing – but no objective thyroid lab abnormalities. *Differentiation:* normal TSH and free T4 with normal reflexes.
- **Splitter:** single feature – **TSH level** – a *raised* TSH points to primary hypothyroidism; a *low/normal* TSH with low T4 points to secondary or euthyroid sick syndrome (but also check cortisol).
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: bradycardia, low voltage (rule out pericardial effusion), prolonged QT interval, T-wave inversion.
  - Capillary glucose: hypoglycaemia can complicate myxoedema coma.
  - Core temperature (tympanic/rectal) if suspicion of myxoedema coma.
- **Bloods:**
  - **TSH and free T4** – essential pair. High TSH + low T4 = overt primary. Normal T4 + raised TSH = subclinical. Low TSH + low T4 = secondary.
  - TPO antibodies (anti-thyroid peroxidase) – positive in Hashimoto’s thyroiditis (≈90%).
  - FBC (anaemia possible), U&E (hyponatraemia), LFT (mild transaminitis), lipid profile (hypercholesterolaemia), glucose.
  - **Cortisol** (9am or random) – mandatory if any suspicion of adrenal insufficiency or secondary hypothyroidism; *do not start thyroid hormone until cortisol known if patient unwell.*
  - CK – elevated in severe hypothyroidism.
- **Imaging:**
  - Thyroid ultrasound if goitre/nodule palpated or for follow-up of known thyroiditis.
  - Pituitary MRI if secondary hypothyroidism suspected (low TSH + low T4 + other pituitary deficits).
- **Special tests:**
  - If myxoedema coma: ABG (hypercapnia, acidosis), blood cultures, CXR (look for infection), cortisol (stat), and consider CSF if meningoencephalitis suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system – diagnosis is biochemical: raised TSH + low free T4 (primary); low TSH + low free T4 (secondary). Clinical severity guides urgency.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – to identify life-threatening bradycardia or pericardial effusion. **Cortisol** – to avoid causing adrenal crisis when starting thyroid hormone (especially in any unwell patient).
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If myxoedema coma suspected: **ABCDE**. Intubate if GCS <8 or respiratory failure. Passive rewarming (do not warm actively/externally – risk of vasodilation and hypotension). IV fluids cautiously (but avoid fluid overload if pericardial effusion).
  - **IV liothyronine (T3)** or **IV levothyroxine (T4)** – dose per [check local policy]. Start only after ensuring *not* in adrenal crisis: give **IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat** before or with first dose of thyroid hormone (to prevent precipitating adrenal crisis).
  - Treat triggering infection (antibiotics after cultures).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start **oral levothyroxine** once patient stable and can swallow – typical starting dose: 25-50 mcg daily (young, healthy, no cardiac disease); start at 12.5-25 mcg daily in elderly or those with IHD [check local policy].
  - Continue IV steroids (hydrocortisone 50-100 mg QDS) until adrenal insufficiency excluded (by cortisol result or synacthen test).
  - Monitor GCS, heart rate, BP, temperature; check ECG, U&E, glucose.
  - If patient on warfarin – thyroid replacement can increase warfarin effect; check INR.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong levothyroxine – adjust dose based on TSH every 6-8 weeks until stable, then annually.
  - Target TSH within normal range (usually 0.5-2.5 mU/L) unless specific circumstances (pregnancy, TSH-secreting tumour).
  - For secondary hypothyroidism, monitor free T4 (not TSH) as pituitary don't respond normal.
  - Refer to endocrinology if difficult to control, secondary hypothyroidism, or pregnancy planning.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reasons):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at standard maintenance (avoid rapid large volumes if myxoedema coma – risk of cardiac overload).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low-molecular-weight heparin unless contraindicated (e.g. active bleeding, but hypothyroidism itself not a contraindication). Use standard weigh-based dose.
  - **Diet/NBM:** no special restrictions; if dysphagia in severe hypothyroidism, consider soft diet/aspiration precautions.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (1-2 hourly if unstable).
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent:** **Levothyroxine (T4) oral** – starting dose:
  - Young (<50 years), healthy, no cardiac disease: 25-50 mcg once daily.
  - Elderly (>65 years), IHD, or severe long-standing hypothyroidism: start at 12.5-25 mcg once daily to avoid precipitating angina/arrhythmia [check local policy].
  - Titrate by 12.5-25 mcg increments every 4-6 weeks until TSH normal.
- **Alternative (short-term / myxoedema coma):**
  - **Liothyronine (T3)** IV – dose per local protocol (typically 5-20 mcg IV bolus then infusion). Do not guess.
  - **Levothyroxine IV** – also available but slower onset; dose per local protocol.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - **Untreated adrenal insufficiency** – absolute contraindication to starting thyroid hormone without steroid cover (risk of adrenal crisis).
  - **Calcium carbonate, iron, sucralfate, bile acid sequestrants** – reduce absorption; separate by at least 4 hours.
  - **Amiodarone, lithium** – can interfere with thyroid function and cause hypothyroidism.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustment needed for renal or hepatic impairment – but start low and titrate cautiously.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting too high a dose in elderly or IHD patients (especially >50 mcg initially) → can trigger angina, atrial fibrillation, or myocardial infarction. **Always start low and go slow.**
- **Second dangerous dose error:** Giving levothyroxine without hydrocortisone in an unwell patient with possible adrenal insufficiency → adrenal crisis. **If in doubt, give steroids first.**
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** **Myxoedema coma** – hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression, seizures, coma; mortality ~20-50%. **Adrenal crisis** (if untreated secondary hypothyroidism). **Pericardial effusion** (can progress to tamponade). **Angina/arrhythmia** from over-replacement.
- **Chronic:** Weight gain, obesity, hypercholesterolaemia (increased CV risk), infertility (anovulation), depression, cognitive decline, dementia-like symptoms, sleep apnoea (from myxoedematous pharyngeal tissue), carpal tunnel syndrome, galactorrhoea (due to increased TRH stimulating prolactin).
sections.Prognosis: - **Excellent** with appropriate levothyroxine replacement – symptoms fully resolve over weeks to months, normal quality of life. **If untreated** → progressive morbidity and risk of myxoedema coma. **Myxoedema coma** carries 20-50% mortality even with prompt treatment.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check cortisol before starting thyroid hormone** in any patient who is unwell, hyponatraemic, or with signs of pituitary disease (hypotension, loss of axillary/pubic hair, decreased libido).
- **Advise patients** to take levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast, and avoid calcium/iron supplements within 4 hours.
- **In pregnancy** – increase levothyroxine dose by ~30-50% once pregnancy confirmed; monitor TSH monthly.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT diagnose hypothyroidism based on TSH alone** – always check free T4 to differentiate subclinical from overt.
- **Do NOT start levothyroxine** without first ensuring adequate adrenal reserve if patient is unwell or has features of secondary hypothyroidism (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia).
- **Do NOT stop levothyroxine abruptly** (can cause recurrence of symptoms and myxoedema coma in severe disease).
- **Do NOT use liothyronine (T3) for routine replacement** – T4 is preferred; T3 only in myxoedema coma or rare situations.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** difficulty breathing, chest pain/palpitations, confusion or altered mental state, severe fatigue or cold intolerance worsening, or any signs of infection (especially if myxoedema coma risk).
- **Follow-up:** GP for repeat TFT in 6-8 weeks after any dose change, then annually once stable. Refer to endocrinology if pregnant, difficult to stabilise, or secondary hypothyroidism.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU immediately** if: GCS <13, NEWS2 ≥5, heart rate <40 bpm, persistent hypotension despite fluids, new arrhythmia, or any suspicion of myxoedema coma (hypothermia, hypoventilation, seizures).
- **Also call if patient starts levothyroxine and develops chest pain or palpitations** – may indicate over-replacement and need to hold dose.
- **Specific thresholds:** Temp <35°C, respiratory rate <8, systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L.

rawMarkdown:
## Hypothyroidism
**One-liner:** Common endocrine deficiency of thyroid hormones; often insidious, but can cause myxoedema coma – a rare but life-threatening emergency.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Myxoedema coma** – hypothermia, bradycardia, altered mental status, hypoventilation; often triggered by infection, trauma, or cold. **Key finding:** core temp <35°C + depressed GCS + bradycardia (not just "tired patient").
- **Adrenal crisis** in undiagnosed secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary failure) – give thyroid hormone without steroids → acute hypotension/collapse. **Key finding:** hyponatraemia + hyperkalaemia + hypotension (rare in primary hypothyroidism; think pituitary).
- **Pericardial effusion/tamponade** – rare but possible in severe chronic hypothyroidism. **Key finding:** low-voltage ECG + distended neck veins + muffled heart sounds + reduced pulse pressure.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain (despite poor appetite), constipation, dry skin, hair loss (especially outer third eyebrows), hoarse voice, cognitive slowing ("brain fog"), menorrhagia (premenopausal).
- **Key exam findings:** bradycardia, delayed relaxation phase of deep tendon reflexes (especially ankle jerk), dry coarse skin, periorbital oedema, goitre (if Hashimoto’s) or absent thyroid (surgical/radioiodine), non-pitting oedema (myxoedema), slow speech, low body temperature.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Subclinical hypothyroidism:** isolated raised TSH, normal free T4 – asymptomatic or mild symptoms. *Differentiation:* labs, no overt signs.
- **Secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary/hypothalamic):** low TSH, low free T4 – may have other pituitary hormone deficiencies (low cortisol, low LH/FSH). *Differentiation:* check TSH – if it is *not* raised, suspect central cause.
- **Euthyroid sick syndrome (non-thyroidal illness):** low T3/T4 but TSH normal or low – occurs in critical illness (sepsis, MI, starvation). *Differentiation:* clinical context – patient is acutely unwell with other organ failure, resolves with treating underlying illness.
- **Depression / chronic fatigue syndrome:** fatigue, weight change, cognitive slowing – but no objective thyroid lab abnormalities. *Differentiation:* normal TSH and free T4 with normal reflexes.
- **Splitter:** single feature – **TSH level** – a *raised* TSH points to primary hypothyroidism; a *low/normal* TSH with low T4 points to secondary or euthyroid sick syndrome (but also check cortisol).

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - ECG: bradycardia, low voltage (rule out pericardial effusion), prolonged QT interval, T-wave inversion.
  - Capillary glucose: hypoglycaemia can complicate myxoedema coma.
  - Core temperature (tympanic/rectal) if suspicion of myxoedema coma.
- **Bloods:**
  - **TSH and free T4** – essential pair. High TSH + low T4 = overt primary. Normal T4 + raised TSH = subclinical. Low TSH + low T4 = secondary.
  - TPO antibodies (anti-thyroid peroxidase) – positive in Hashimoto’s thyroiditis (≈90%).
  - FBC (anaemia possible), U&E (hyponatraemia), LFT (mild transaminitis), lipid profile (hypercholesterolaemia), glucose.
  - **Cortisol** (9am or random) – mandatory if any suspicion of adrenal insufficiency or secondary hypothyroidism; *do not start thyroid hormone until cortisol known if patient unwell.*
  - CK – elevated in severe hypothyroidism.
- **Imaging:**
  - Thyroid ultrasound if goitre/nodule palpated or for follow-up of known thyroiditis.
  - Pituitary MRI if secondary hypothyroidism suspected (low TSH + low T4 + other pituitary deficits).
- **Special tests:**
  - If myxoedema coma: ABG (hypercapnia, acidosis), blood cultures, CXR (look for infection), cortisol (stat), and consider CSF if meningoencephalitis suspected.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** No single scoring system – diagnosis is biochemical: raised TSH + low free T4 (primary); low TSH + low free T4 (secondary). Clinical severity guides urgency.
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** – to identify life-threatening bradycardia or pericardial effusion. **Cortisol** – to avoid causing adrenal crisis when starting thyroid hormone (especially in any unwell patient).

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If myxoedema coma suspected: **ABCDE**. Intubate if GCS <8 or respiratory failure. Passive rewarming (do not warm actively/externally – risk of vasodilation and hypotension). IV fluids cautiously (but avoid fluid overload if pericardial effusion).
  - **IV liothyronine (T3)** or **IV levothyroxine (T4)** – dose per [check local policy]. Start only after ensuring *not* in adrenal crisis: give **IV hydrocortisone 100 mg stat** before or with first dose of thyroid hormone (to prevent precipitating adrenal crisis).
  - Treat triggering infection (antibiotics after cultures).
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Start **oral levothyroxine** once patient stable and can swallow – typical starting dose: 25-50 mcg daily (young, healthy, no cardiac disease); start at 12.5-25 mcg daily in elderly or those with IHD [check local policy].
  - Continue IV steroids (hydrocortisone 50-100 mg QDS) until adrenal insufficiency excluded (by cortisol result or synacthen test).
  - Monitor GCS, heart rate, BP, temperature; check ECG, U&E, glucose.
  - If patient on warfarin – thyroid replacement can increase warfarin effect; check INR.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Lifelong levothyroxine – adjust dose based on TSH every 6-8 weeks until stable, then annually.
  - Target TSH within normal range (usually 0.5-2.5 mU/L) unless specific circumstances (pregnancy, TSH-secreting tumour).
  - For secondary hypothyroidism, monitor free T4 (not TSH) as pituitary don't respond normal.
  - Refer to endocrinology if difficult to control, secondary hypothyroidism, or pregnancy planning.
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reasons):**
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% saline at standard maintenance (avoid rapid large volumes if myxoedema coma – risk of cardiac overload).
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** low-molecular-weight heparin unless contraindicated (e.g. active bleeding, but hypothyroidism itself not a contraindication). Use standard weigh-based dose.
  - **Diet/NBM:** no special restrictions; if dysphagia in severe hypothyroidism, consider soft diet/aspiration precautions.
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 4-hourly (1-2 hourly if unstable).

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent:** **Levothyroxine (T4) oral** – starting dose:
  - Young (<50 years), healthy, no cardiac disease: 25-50 mcg once daily.
  - Elderly (>65 years), IHD, or severe long-standing hypothyroidism: start at 12.5-25 mcg once daily to avoid precipitating angina/arrhythmia [check local policy].
  - Titrate by 12.5-25 mcg increments every 4-6 weeks until TSH normal.
- **Alternative (short-term / myxoedema coma):**
  - **Liothyronine (T3)** IV – dose per local protocol (typically 5-20 mcg IV bolus then infusion). Do not guess.
  - **Levothyroxine IV** – also available but slower onset; dose per local protocol.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - **Untreated adrenal insufficiency** – absolute contraindication to starting thyroid hormone without steroid cover (risk of adrenal crisis).
  - **Calcium carbonate, iron, sucralfate, bile acid sequestrants** – reduce absorption; separate by at least 4 hours.
  - **Amiodarone, lithium** – can interfere with thyroid function and cause hypothyroidism.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No specific dose adjustment needed for renal or hepatic impairment – but start low and titrate cautiously.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Starting too high a dose in elderly or IHD patients (especially >50 mcg initially) → can trigger angina, atrial fibrillation, or myocardial infarction. **Always start low and go slow.**
- **Second dangerous dose error:** Giving levothyroxine without hydrocortisone in an unwell patient with possible adrenal insufficiency → adrenal crisis. **If in doubt, give steroids first.**

### Complications
- **Acute:** **Myxoedema coma** – hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression, seizures, coma; mortality ~20-50%. **Adrenal crisis** (if untreated secondary hypothyroidism). **Pericardial effusion** (can progress to tamponade). **Angina/arrhythmia** from over-replacement.
- **Chronic:** Weight gain, obesity, hypercholesterolaemia (increased CV risk), infertility (anovulation), depression, cognitive decline, dementia-like symptoms, sleep apnoea (from myxoedematous pharyngeal tissue), carpal tunnel syndrome, galactorrhoea (due to increased TRH stimulating prolactin).

### Prognosis
- **Excellent** with appropriate levothyroxine replacement – symptoms fully resolve over weeks to months, normal quality of life. **If untreated** → progressive morbidity and risk of myxoedema coma. **Myxoedema coma** carries 20-50% mortality even with prompt treatment.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check cortisol before starting thyroid hormone** in any patient who is unwell, hyponatraemic, or with signs of pituitary disease (hypotension, loss of axillary/pubic hair, decreased libido).
- **Advise patients** to take levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast, and avoid calcium/iron supplements within 4 hours.
- **In pregnancy** – increase levothyroxine dose by ~30-50% once pregnancy confirmed; monitor TSH monthly.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT diagnose hypothyroidism based on TSH alone** – always check free T4 to differentiate subclinical from overt.
- **Do NOT start levothyroxine** without first ensuring adequate adrenal reserve if patient is unwell or has features of secondary hypothyroidism (hypotension, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia).
- **Do NOT stop levothyroxine abruptly** (can cause recurrence of symptoms and myxoedema coma in severe disease).
- **Do NOT use liothyronine (T3) for routine replacement** – T4 is preferred; T3 only in myxoedema coma or rare situations.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** difficulty breathing, chest pain/palpitations, confusion or altered mental state, severe fatigue or cold intolerance worsening, or any signs of infection (especially if myxoedema coma risk).
- **Follow-up:** GP for repeat TFT in 6-8 weeks after any dose change, then annually once stable. Refer to endocrinology if pregnant, difficult to stabilise, or secondary hypothyroidism.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU immediately** if: GCS <13, NEWS2 ≥5, heart rate <40 bpm, persistent hypotension despite fluids, new arrhythmia, or any suspicion of myxoedema coma (hypothermia, hypoventilation, seizures).
- **Also call if patient starts levothyroxine and develops chest pain or palpitations** – may indicate over-replacement and need to hold dose.
- **Specific thresholds:** Temp <35°C, respiratory rate <8, systolic BP <90 mmHg, lactate >4 mmol/L.
